sections.heading: Core Concepts
sections.content: A **Heap** is conceptually a `[[primary|complete binary tree]]`, yet often described as "an **array** in binary tree's clothing." Its essence lies in maintaining a strict parent-child ordering to ensure the most efficient retrieval of the `[[primary|extreme value]]`.
items: Stored in a one-dimensional **array**; tree structure is simulated via simple `[[primary|index arithmetic]]` (e.g., left child at 2i+1), eliminating pointer overhead
Like a `[[primary|tournament bracket]]`, each node is the winner over everything below it, so the **root node** always holds the global extreme value
Must satisfy the `[[primary|heap property]]`: every **parent node**'s value is always greater than or equal to (or less than or equal to) its **child nodes**

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: Heap achieves an efficient balance between reading and updating the extreme value. It maintains the heap property with only a small number of comparisons and swaps along the tree height, without the cost of a full re-sort.
items: **Peek extreme**: [[success|O(1)]] — the max or min is always at the root (index 0) and can be read directly
**Insert element**: [[success|O(log n)]] — the new element is appended to the end, then bubbled up by comparing and swapping with its parent along the tree height
**Delete extreme**: [[success|O(log n)]] — the root is removed and replaced by the last element, then sifted down by comparing and swapping along the tree height

sections.heading: What It Optimizes
sections.content: A **Heap** is optimized for retrieving the extreme value as efficiently as possible, but makes trade-offs for other operations. Understanding its strengths and weaknesses helps you switch to a more suitable advanced structure when you hit a performance bottleneck.
items: **Excels at priority**: ideal for solving **Top-k** problems, implementing a **priority queue**, or powering shortest-path algorithms
**Poor at arbitrary search**: finding a specific node is inefficient; implementations often need an auxiliary **dictionary** for fast lookups
**Poor at merging and decreasing keys**: frequent heap merges or key-decrease operations are costly — consider a **Fibonacci heap** or **pairing heap** instead

sections.heading: When to Use It
sections.content: A Heap is the ideal choice whenever you need to repeatedly fetch or update the extreme value in a dynamic dataset, or when handling k-way merges and priority-based scheduling.
items: **Top-k problems**: find the k-th largest element or the top-100 most frequent items without a full sort
**Priority queue**: when a system must dynamically decide execution order by priority (e.g., schedulers or event simulators)
**Shortest-path algorithms**: in Dijkstra's algorithm, repeatedly extracting the unvisited vertex closest to the source
**K-way merge**: efficiently merging multiple sorted streams (e.g., multiple timestamped log files) into a single sorted output

sections.heading: Array–Tree Mapping
sections.content: Beginners are often confused that a Heap is conceptually a **binary tree** yet implemented as an **array**. This section clarifies the distinction and covers the elegant **index arithmetic** for navigating parent and child nodes.
items: **Structure mapping**: a Heap is a **complete binary tree** stored contiguously in an **array** — a binary tree in array's clothing
**Locating the root**: the tree's **root node** is always stored at index `[[primary|0]]`
**Finding children**: for a node at index i, its left child is at `[[primary|2i+1]]` and its right child at `[[primary|2i+2]]`
**Finding the parent**: for a node at index i, its parent is found via integer division at `[[primary|(i-1)//2]]`

sections.heading: Priority Queue vs. Heap
sections.content: Beginners often confuse a **priority queue** (an abstract data type) with a **Heap** (a concrete data structure). Clarifying the distinction builds a correct mental model of data structures.
items: **Abstract vs. concrete**: a **priority queue** defines the contract for extracting the extreme value; a **Heap** is the structure that fulfills it efficiently
**Implementation flexibility**: a **priority queue** can also be implemented with an array or linked list — a **Heap** simply provides the best complexity
**Core focus**: a **priority queue** is about the management contract; a **Heap** pursues [[success|O(log n)]] operational performance
